__data__.text: UNIVERSITY INSTITUTE OF ENGINEERING Department Of Computer Science & Engineering Bachelor of Computer Science 6th Semester System Design (23CSH-314/23ITH-314) Unit No. 1 Chapter No. 1.3.1 Lecture No.5 Topic : SOLID Principles Mayan (E18387) Technical Trainer(CSE) Academic Session 2025-26 EVEN Semester Jan-Jun 2026

Learning Objectives 1. Explain each SOLID principle with real-time examples. 2. Identify violations of SOLID principles in existing code designs. 3. Apply SOLID principles to design clean low-level components. 4. Analyze design decisions using SOLID guidelines. 5. Relate SOLID principles to system design and scalable architectures.

Topics 1. SOLID Principle 2. Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) 3. Open-Closed Principle (OCP) 4. Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) 5. Interface Segregation Principle (ISP) 6. Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)

Introduction to SOLID Principles SOLID principles are a set of five object-oriented design guidelines that help developers write maintainable, scalable, and flexible software. These principles were introduced by Robert C. Martin (Uncle Bob) and are widely used in: • Enterprise applications • System design interviews • Framework and library design SOLID principles are essential for clean Low-Level Design (LLD).

Overview of SOLID Principles SOLID stands for : ❖ S – Single Responsibility Principle ❖ O – Open/Closed Principle ❖ L – Liskov Substitution Principle ❖ I – Interface Segregation Principle ❖ D – Dependency Inversion Principle Each principle addresses a specific design problem in object-oriented systems.

Why SOLID Principles Matter Without SOLID principles, software systems often become: ✓ Difficult to modify ✓ Error-prone ✓ Hard to test ✓ Tightly coupled SOLID principles help: ✓ Reduce code complexity ✓ Improve readability ✓ Support future changes ✓ Align code with real-world requirements They are heavily used in microservices, APIs, and scalable systems.

Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) Definition: A class should have only one reason to change, meaning it should have only one responsibility. In simple terms: ❑ Each class should have only one responsibility ❑ Each class should have only one axis of change ❑ Responsibility = "reason to change" One class → One job SRP improves clarity and reduces the impact of changes.

SRP – Real-Time Example SRP Violation - "God Class" Problem What's Wrong with This Employee Class? Three Different Responsibilities Mixed Together: Business Logic Layer ❖ calculateSalary() ❖ calculateTax() ❖ Reason to change: Business rule changes Data Access Layer ❖ saveToDatabase() ❖ loadFromDatabase() ❖ Reason to change: Database schema/technology changes Presentation Layer ❖ printReport() ❖ generatePDF() ❖ Reason to change: UI/Report format changes

SRP – Refactored Solution: Four Specialized Classes, Each with ONE Job: Data Model (POJO) └─ Employee Class - Only stores data (id, name, salary) - Only getters/setters Responsibility: Represent employee data Reason to change: Data structure changes Business Logic └─ EmployeeCalculator Class - calculateSalary() - calculateTax() Responsibility: Perform calculations Reason to change: Tax/salary formula changes Data Access └─ EmployeeRepository Class - save(Employee emp) - load(int id) Responsibility: Persist/retrieve data Reason to change: Database technology changes Presentation └─ EmployeeReport Class - print(Employee emp) - generatePDF(Employee emp) Responsibility: Format and display data Reason to change: Report format/UI changes

Benefits of SRP ▪ Easier to understand - each class does one thing ▪ Easier to test - focused test cases ▪ Reduced coupling - changes affect fewer components ▪ Easier maintenance - isolate changes to specific areas

Open/Closed Principle (OCP) Definition: Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification. This means: ❑ New behavior should be added without changing existing code ❑ OCP reduces the risk of introducing bugs when adding new features. OCP – Real-Time Example Violation Example: A PaymentService with multiple if-else conditions for payment types. OCP-Compliant Design: ❑ Create a Payment interface ❑ Implement CardPayment, UPIPayment, WalletPayment ❑ Add new payment types without modifying existing code This approach is common in payment gateways and plugin systems.
Violation Example:
OCP Implementation Patterns Solution Using Abstraction:
Benefits of OCP ✓ Safe feature extension ✓ Better scalability ✓ Cleaner code structure ✓ Reduced regression bugs ✓ OCP is essential in systems that evolve frequently

Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) Definition: ▪ Objects of a superclass should be replaceable with objects of its subclasses without affecting correctness. In simple terms: ▪ Child classes should behave like their parent class. ❖ LSP ensures behavioral consistency. Core Concept: ▪ "Objects of a superclass should be replaceable with objects of its subclasses without breaking the application.” Formal Definition: ▪ If S is a subtype of T, then objects of type T may be replaced with objects of type S without altering any of the desirable properties of the program.
Violation Example (Classic Rectangle-Square Problem):
Refactored Solution:
Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) Benefits of LSP 1. Reliable polymorphism 2. Predictable behavior 3. Fewer runtime errors 4. LSP is critical when designing inheritance hierarchies.

Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) Design by Contract: ❑ Preconditions cannot be strengthened in subclass ❑ Postconditions cannot be weakened in subclass ❑ Invariants must be preserved in subclass ❑ History constraint - subclass shouldn't allow state changes parent prohibits Benefits of LSP ❑ Reliable polymorphism ❑ Predictable behavior ❑ Fewer runtime errors ❑ LSP is critical when designing inheritance hierarchies.

Interface Segregation Principle (ISP) Definition: Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they do not use. In simple terms: ▪ Many small interfaces are better than one large interface. ▪ ISP avoids unnecessary implementation. Core Concept: ❖ "Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they do not use.” What It Means: ❖ Split large interfaces into smaller, specific ones ❖ Avoid "fat" or "polluted" interfaces ❖ Prevent classes from implementing irrelevant methods
Violation Example:
ISP Implementation
Interface Segregation Principle (ISP) Benefits of ISP ➢ Reduced coupling ➢ Cleaner interfaces ➢ Easier implementation ➢ Improved flexibility ➢ ISP is widely used in API and SDK design.

Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP) Definition: ❑ High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules. ❑ Both should depend on abstractions. ❑ Abstractions should not depend on details; details should depend on abstractions. Core Concept: ❑ "High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules. Both should depend on abstractions.” Two Key Rules: 1. Abstractions should not depend on details. Details should depend on abstractions. 2. High-level policy should not depend on low-level details. This approach enables loose coupling and testability.

When to Apply SOLID Principles Appropriate Contexts: ❑ Large codebases with multiple developers ❑ Long-lived projects requiring maintenance ❑ Frameworks and libraries used by others ❑ Complex business domains with changing requirements When to Be Pragmatic: ❑ Prototypes and MVPs - focus on speed ❑ Small scripts/tools - simplicity over structure ❑ Performance-critical sections - abstractions have cost ❑ Proven stable code - "if it ain't broke, don't fix it" Incremental Adoption Strategy: 1. Start with SRP - easiest to understand and apply 2. Add DIP with DI - enables testability 3. Apply ISP when interfaces become bloated 4. Use OCP when adding similar features 5. Ensure LSP when creating inheritance hierarchies

SOLID & Design Patterns Relationship Design Pattern Primary SOLID Principle Also Supports Strategy OCP, DIP SRP, ISP Decorator OCP SRP Observer OCP, DIP SRP Factory Method DIP, OCP SRP Adapter ISP, DIP SRP Facade SRP DIP Proxy DIP, ISP SRP Template Method OCP SRP, LSP Patterns as SOLID Solutions: ❑ Patterns provide proven implementations of SOLID principles ❑ SOLID provides theoretical foundation for patterns ❑ Together they form robust OOP design methodology

Applications of SOLID Principles SOLID principles are applied in: ❑ Enterprise applications ❑ Microservices architecture ❑ API and framework design ❑ Scalable backend systems ❑ Large codebases They improve long-term system health.

Summary of the Lecture In this lecture, we learned: ➢ Meaning and importance of SOLID principles ➢ Real-time examples of each principle ➢ How SOLID improves low-level design ➢ Why SOLID is essential for scalable systems ➢ Applying SOLID leads to clean, maintainable code.

Quiz / Check Your Understanding 1. Why is SRP important in large systems? 2. How does OCP prevent regression bugs? 3. What problem does LSP solve? 4. Why is ISP preferred over large interfaces? 5. How does DIP support testability?

Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) Q: Are SOLID principles mandatory? A: No, but strongly recommended for maintainable systems. Q: Can SOLID principles be violated sometimes? A: Yes, if justified by performance or simplicity needs. Q: Are SOLID principles used in system design interviews? A: Yes, frequently.
